key_exchanges.speaker: Euthyphro
propositions_asserted: Socrates has left the Lyceum
Socrates is at the Porch of the King Archon
propositions_presupposed: Socrates is not usually involved in legal suits

key_exchanges.speaker: Socrates
propositions_asserted: Meletus is prosecuting Socrates for corrupting the youth
Meletus accuses Socrates of making new gods and denying old ones
Meletus is right to care for the virtue of the young first
propositions_presupposed: The state is the judge of political and moral conduct

key_exchanges.speaker: Euthyphro
propositions_asserted: Attacking Socrates is attacking the foundation of the state
Meletus is attacking Socrates because of his familiar sign
The public is jealous of those with divine knowledge
propositions_presupposed: Divine knowledge is a real and specialized field

key_exchanges.speaker: Euthyphro
propositions_asserted: Euthyphro is prosecuting his father for murder
The victim was a dependant field-labourer who killed a servant
Pollution from murder is the same regardless of whether the victim is a relative or a stranger
His family is wrong to think a son should not prosecute a father
propositions_presupposed: Piety involves a specific set of rules that outweigh family loyalty

key_exchanges.speaker: Euthyphro
propositions_asserted: Euthyphro has exact knowledge of all matters regarding piety and impiety
This exact knowledge is what distinguishes Euthyphro from other men
propositions_presupposed: Piety is a subject that can be known with exactness

key_exchanges.speaker: Socrates
propositions_asserted: Piety is always the same in every action
Impiety has one notion which includes everything impious
propositions_presupposed: There is a unified essence to piety

key_exchanges.speaker: Euthyphro
propositions_conceded: Piety is consistent and has a single defining idea

key_exchanges.speaker: Euthyphro
propositions_asserted: Piety is doing as I am doing: prosecuting the guilty
Not to prosecute the guilty is impiety
Zeus is the best and most righteous of the gods
Zeus bound his father Cronos for a crime
propositions_presupposed: The actions of the gods provide a moral precedent for humans

key_exchanges.speaker: Socrates
propositions_asserted: There are many pious acts other than prosecuting a father
A definition should explain the general idea/standard of piety
propositions_presupposed: An example is not a definition

key_exchanges.speaker: Euthyphro
propositions_conceded: There is one idea that makes the pious pious
A general standard for measuring actions is required

key_exchanges.speaker: Euthyphro
propositions_asserted: Piety is that which is dear to the gods
Impiety is that which is not dear to the gods

key_exchanges.speaker: Socrates
propositions_asserted: The gods have enmities and differences
Enmity is caused by differences about the just, the good, and the honorable
The gods disagree about what is just and good
The same things are loved by some gods and hated by others
propositions_presupposed: The gods are subject to human-like disputes over values

key_exchanges.speaker: Euthyphro
propositions_conceded: The same things can be both pious and impious if gods disagree
The previous definition leads to a contradiction

key_exchanges.speaker: Euthyphro
propositions_asserted: All gods agree that a murderer should be punished
propositions_presupposed: There are some universal moral agreements among gods

key_exchanges.speaker: Socrates
propositions_asserted: People and gods do not argue about whether the guilty should be punished
People and gods argue about who is guilty in a specific case

key_exchanges.speaker: Socrates
propositions_asserted: What all the gods hate is impious
What all the gods love is pious

key_exchanges.speaker: Euthyphro
propositions_asserted: What all the gods love is pious and holy
propositions_conceded: The definition requires the unanimous love of the gods

key_exchanges.speaker: Socrates
propositions_asserted: The pious is loved by the gods because it is pious
It is not pious because it is loved
The 'dear to the gods' is dear because it is loved by them
Piety and 'being loved by the gods' are distinct concepts
propositions_presupposed: An essential definition is different from an accidental attribute

key_exchanges.speaker: Euthyphro
propositions_conceded: A thing is loved because it is holy
The attribute of being loved does not explain the nature of holiness
Arguments seem to move away and not stay fixed

key_exchanges.speaker: Socrates
propositions_asserted: The pious is necessarily just
Not everything that is just is pious
Piety is a part of justice

key_exchanges.speaker: Euthyphro
propositions_conceded: Piety is a subset of justice

key_exchanges.speaker: Euthyphro
propositions_asserted: Piety is the part of justice which attends to the gods
The other part of justice attends to men

key_exchanges.speaker: Socrates
propositions_asserted: Attention usually aims at the good or benefit of the attended
Pious acts do not benefit or improve the gods

key_exchanges.speaker: Euthyphro
propositions_conceded: Piety is not attention that improves the gods
propositions_presupposed: Gods are already perfect and cannot be improved

key_exchanges.speaker: Euthyphro
propositions_asserted: Piety is a kind of ministration or service to the gods
Piety is learning how to please the gods in word and deed
Prayer and sacrifice are the means of this pleasing
Such piety is the salvation of families and states

key_exchanges.speaker: Socrates
propositions_asserted: Piety is a science of asking and giving
Sacrificing is giving to the gods; prayer is asking of them
Piety is a sort of business between gods and men

key_exchanges.speaker: Euthyphro
propositions_asserted: Gifts to gods are tributes of honor and things that please them
propositions_conceded: Piety is what is pleasing or dear to the gods

key_exchanges.speaker: Socrates
propositions_asserted: The argument has come round in a circle
The current position (piety = dear to gods) contradicts the earlier conclusion

key_exchanges.speaker: Euthyphro
propositions_conceded: One of the two contradictory positions must be true

key_exchanges.speaker: Socrates
propositions_asserted: We must begin again to ask what piety is
Euthyphro must know the nature of piety to have risked prosecuting his father

key_exchanges.speaker: Euthyphro
propositions_asserted: He is in a hurry and must go
